author: Anup Chavan
description: Generate adaptive practice questions from your notes and PDFs using your own AI provider. Spaced repetition scheduling, difficulty that adapts to your answers, and blind answer verification. - This plugin has not been manually reviewed by Obsidian staff.

manifest.author: Anup Chavan
manifest.description: Adaptive spaced practice from your notes, PDFs, and media using your own model provider.
manifest.name: Adaptive Practice

name: Adaptive Practice

releases.author: anupchavan
releases.description: - Sessions now deliver the full requested question count: when background answer-verification retracts a contested question, the next flow batch grows to replace it

releases.author: anupchavan
releases.description:
- Flow: skipped questions no longer drag accuracy down — 3 correct out of 4 answered now reads as 75% and raises difficulty (single step from 75%, double step when fast and ≥85%)
- Flow: difficulty adjustments now compound across batches within a session (a strong run climbs 50→60→70 instead of hovering)
- Flow: two skips in the window still ease difficulty (overload signal)

releases.author: anupchavan
releases.description:
- Daily selection: notes from the last 48h now lead in ascending created order (oldest new note first; filesystem ctime breaks frontmatter date ties)
- Older never-practiced notes are shuffled per day instead of always surfacing in the same order (stable within a day, rotates daily)
- Fixed a redundant type assertion in difficulty calibration; unknown model difficulty labels remain demote-only

releases.author: anupchavan
releases.description:
- Difficulty labels now come from the model's calibration rubric; the keyword heuristic can only demote fake-hard recall, never promote wordy questions — fixes random-looking tags
- Flow now escalates on sustained accuracy alone (double-step when answers are also fast) — consistently correct sessions get harder instead of staying easy

releases.author: anupchavan
releases.description:
- Forgetting-curve fix: notes created today/yesterday now lead daily practice selection (newest first), riding above the new-material throttle — previously a backlog of old never-practiced notes could bury exactly the notes whose recall decays fastest
- Older never-practiced notes remain capped so review debt still gets its slots

releases.author: anupchavan
releases.description:
- Questions still to be generated now appear as dashed slots in the question grid, with a tooltip on hover — the caption below the grid is gone
- (from 0.6.5's line) difficulty calibration: easy questions are now genuinely easy — crisp, trap-free, answerable right after reading the note

releases.author: anupchavan
releases.description:
- Sessions start as soon as the first questions are ready: answer verification now runs in the background and quietly retracts contested questions you haven't reached (roughly halves time-to-first-question)
- Verification derivations are compact, trimming generation cost and latency
- Fixed duplicate dashboard tab after plugin updates (existing leaf is reused; stragglers are deduped)

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description: Bumped version and fixed lint errors.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Fixed

- **"true" section headings in settings on Obsidian 1.13.** The declarative group schema takes the heading *text* in the `heading` field; 0.6.0 passed a boolean, so every group rendered a literal "true" heading and logged `duplicate setting key "group:true"` on every open. Groups now carry their real names and the console stays quiet.

## Changed

- **The topic picker now looks and behaves like a native settings list** (the hotkeys-panel treatment): a proper search field with built-in clear button, filter chips (All / Due / New / Low skill / PDFs) in the native chip style, the course/folder dropdown beside them, and each note as a real settings row — checkbox on the left, title with PDF/folder badges, path and aliases as the description, skill and due status on the right. Rows and inputs are built from Obsidian's own components (Setting, SearchComponent, DropdownComponent), so themes style them for free.
- **License changed to MIT.** The plugin is fully open source, effective now.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
Two more analytical moves mined from a top-tier physics problem book, and a full pass over the plugin's chrome: settings, modals, and notices now speak fluent Obsidian.

## Added

- **Two new analytical moves (catalog: 24)**, mined from Pathfinder-style olympiad exercises and domain-general as always: **instrument vs reality** — a quantity known only through a recording process that systematically distorts it (delay, relative reference, sampling bias); recover the truth from the recording or predict the recording, against distractors that quietly equate the two — and **tight bounds** — inputs known only within ranges; derive the tightest range or extreme value the data licenses, against falsely precise points and looser-than-needed bounds.
- **Model picker.** The model name is now a dropdown of known-good models per provider (provider default marked), with a Custom option that reveals a free-text field — no more typing exact model IDs from memory.
- **Declarative settings on Obsidian 1.13+.** The settings tab now provides setting definitions to newer Obsidian versions, which makes every option findable from the global settings search; older versions keep the classic tab unchanged (minimum app version stays 1.11.4).

## Changed

- **Note filters upgraded** to the newer engine: new operators (is exactly, does not start/end with), new fields (basename, extension, outgoing links), inline suggestions for files, folders, tags, and property values, wikilink-aware value pills, and keyboard-friendly editing. Existing saved filters keep working unchanged.
- **Notices got a proper visual language**: a small plugin label, the message, and a right-aligned action bar with accent-styled primary buttons — replacing the crammed grey buttons. Notices also no longer swallow clicks around them.
- **Modals now use Obsidian's official building blocks** (modal titles, section headings, button rows) instead of hand-rolled headings and buttons, so they follow your theme correctly.
- Settings copy rewritten to Obsidian's conventions: shorter names, sentence case, concise descriptions.

## Fixed

- **Answering the last buffered question mid-generation no longer ends the session.** The action button now shows a "Generating next question…" loading state with a spinner and re-enables the moment the next flow batch lands.
- Verified the daily reminder end to end (minute-level check, time gate, once-a-day and cooldown suppression, draft/generation guards) — it works; its notice simply looked bad, and now doesn't.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Fixed

- **High-skill sessions no longer die with "Failed to generate enough challenging questions."** Any note above skill 80 put the whole session behind a strict difficulty gate: zero easy questions allowed per high-skill topic, a minimum hard count, subtopic spread — and if two repair attempts couldn't satisfy all of it, the plugin threw the entire session away. One question that the difficulty estimator relabeled "easy" was enough. Difficulty targets now shape effort instead of gating service: the challenge-repair requests still fire (so the model is still pushed to produce harder questions), but whatever the strongest available batch is, it ships — with the shortfall logged to the console. A slightly-softer session beats an error toast every time, and the in-session flow controller adapts difficulty from your results regardless.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Added

- **Double edge** (move #22), mined from the actual Hennessy & Patterson chapter-2 exercise set: a single design change argued through *both* of its mechanisms — how it helps AND how the very same change hurts, or the condition deciding which edge dominates (H&P 2.16.3: "how could this change decrease program size? How could it increase it?"). Generalizes to caching, indexing, dosage, policy — any one-change-two-mechanisms setup.

## Changed

- **Counterexample hunt got stricter**: where the material supports it, a broken universal claim now demands the minimal breaking case *or the exact range of inputs where it fails* (H&P 2.11 style: "what range of values causes overflow?"), never a hand-wave that it "sometimes" fails.
- Validation note: mapping the full exercise set confirmed 0.5.2's three moves are the backbone of problem-book style — over half of H&P chapter 2's exercises are faithful-translation or resource-minimality instances, and "what do we know for certain" (2.33) is necessary-vs-possible verbatim.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Added

- **Three new analytical moves**, mined from the reasoning patterns of classic architecture problem sets (Hennessy & Patterson chapter exercises) and, as always, domain-general:
  - **Faithful translation** — the same content in two representations (formula↔code, spec↔implementation, source↔target language): produce the faithful counterpart, or spot the one subtle infidelity among near-miss translations.
  - **Resource minimality** — what is the *minimum* of a bounded resource (steps, memory, queries, assumptions) that still works; distractors are correct-but-wasteful or infeasibly tight.
  - **Capacity limit** — a representation has a fixed budget (bits, range, precision, slots): what exactly fits, what happens one step past the limit, and which workaround the system needs beyond it.
- **Move weighting by material kind.** Procedural/formal notes lean on translation, minimality, capacity, boundaries, and flawed arguments; conceptual notes lean on false symmetry, minimal pairs, and causal direction; factual notes on necessary-vs-possible and comparisons. The model now picks moves with the note's epistemic family in mind. Catalog: 21 moves.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
Live-feedback release: the first real sessions on 0.5.0 (Anthropic + Gemini) showed exactly where the analytical moves lost their teeth. All fixes are domain-general.

## Changed

- **Traps are experienced, not narrated.** The moves kept collapsing into one template: "A learner argues X. Why is this wrong?" — the stem discloses the verdict, so the learner articulates someone else's mistake instead of risking their own. New rules: a stem may present a claim, derivation, or approach but never reveals whether it is correct; "why is this wrong" phrasing is banned; the "someone argues" frame is capped at one per session. The deep-authoring attack pass now hunts verdict leaks too.
- **A hard question must leave two options standing.** Distractors were one plausible answer plus three eliminable-by-tone. Hard MCQs now require at least two options that are conclusions of complete, plausible reasoning chains diverging at exactly one step — the classic form being the same claim with the direction, boundary, or quantifier flipped. If elimination works without touching the mechanism, the question isn't hard.
- **Re-skins count as repeats.** One session asked the same network-vs-Internet question three times wearing different city names. Continuation and top-up prompts now define a repeat as the same point in a new costume; a second question on a covered concept must probe a different aspect through a different move.
- **The moves are enforced from the user prompt as well.** One provider ignored the system-prompt catalog entirely and produced twenty definitional questions. Every medium and hard question is now explicitly required to instantiate a move; "what is X" phrasing is reserved for easy questions on genuinely factual material.
- **Explanations open by naming the trap** — when a question has a tempting wrong path, the explanation's first sentence says what it was and why it fails, so a wrong answer teaches the pattern, not just the fact.

## Fixed

- **Source-note links in stems gave the answer's category away.** Two culprits: a leftover 0.4.3 calibration step still injected `[[wikilinks]]` to source notes into stems whenever the stem mentioned the topic title (removed — 0.4.4 had reversed this decision everywhere else), and model-written links that resolve to real notes slipped past the render guard, which only demoted dead links. All internal links in question content are now unwrapped to plain text at parse time; image embeds, external URLs, and code spans (bash `[[ -f x ]]` stays intact) are untouched. The source note still appears after you answer.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Added

- **Analytical moves engine.** The reasoning patterns behind great problem-book questions aren't magic, they're *moves* — reusable maneuvers like approximation breakdown (put the learner where a reflexive idealization stops holding), false symmetry (two views that look interchangeable until a deeper level splits them), necessary-vs-possible (what can you *certainly* conclude?), minimal pair (two setups differing in one feature with different outcomes), limiting case, counterexample hunt, flawed argument, hidden assumption, and ten more. Generation now carries this 18-move catalog and instructs the model to pick the move the material genuinely supports and instantiate it on your note — instead of "write a hard question", which regresses to competent-but-flat. The moves key off *structural* features of the material (a rule with assumptions, a claimed universal, steps that might not commute), never a subject, so the same catalog drives code, math, medicine, law, history, or physics notes. It lives in the cached system prompt, so it costs almost nothing per session.
- **Deep authoring (optional).** A second, adversarial pass over each batch's medium and hard questions: the model attacks its own drafts — can a test-wise learner shortcut the reasoning? is the trap actually tempting and actually wrong? does "certainly" truly hold? do length or wording give the answer away? — and re-emits sharpened rewrites. This roughly doubles tokens per session, so it is off by default. The pass is fail-safe: rewrites the model drops or mangles keep their originals, a failed request keeps the whole batch, and the sharpened set still goes through blind answer verification before it reaches you.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Added

- **Cross-topic synthesis, grounded.** When a session holds two or more notes, the plugin computes their *structural* connections from the existing index — notes that link to each other, shared tags, shared concept candidates — and hands the model a tiny bridge list with permission to write 1–2 synthesis questions that need both topics, only where a bridge is real. No bridge, no block, no forced analogies, ~zero extra tokens.

## Changed

- **Stems got their brevity back.** 0.4.3's self-containment rule caused paragraph-length questions. The rule now demands self-contained *and* terse: define what's used, 1–3 short sentences of context, cut every word that doesn't change the answer, and one question = one ask (no more "which statement identifies X *and also explains* Y").
- **The source note is revealed after answering, not before.** The "From [[note]]" chip moved into the result panel: naming the topic up front hands you the concept's category, and figuring out which concept applies is part of the exercise. The prompt no longer asks for inline links at all, and instructs the model to name the tested concept only when the question is unanswerable without it.

## Fixed

- **Links to notes that don't exist.** Models invented wikilinks to concepts ("[[Circuit Switching]]") that aren't notes, rendering as "click to create" bait. Unresolved internal links in questions, options, and explanations are now unwrapped to plain text at render time, and the prompt forbids model-written links entirely.
- **Longest-option-is-correct giveaway.** New hard rule: options must be homogeneous in form, reasoning depth, and length (within ~25%), written by drafting the correct option first and matching each distractor to it. The built-in format exemplar itself modeled this bias (its correct option was the longest) — fixed, its correct option is now the shortest. The eval metrics also track the giveaway per question (`correctLongestOption`) so provider comparisons can measure it.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Added

- **Answer verification (on by default).** Every generated batch is re-solved *blind* — the model gets the questions back with answers hidden and must derive each one independently. Questions whose marked answer fails the check are dropped, so a wrong answer key can no longer mark your correct choice incorrect. Costs about one extra request per batch; toggle under Settings → Verify answers. If the verifier contests more than half a batch, the verifier run is distrusted and the batch is kept.
- **Source-note link on every question.** A "From [[note]]" chip renders under the question header (clickable, with hover preview), so the source is one click away even when the model forgot to link it inline. The prompt now also asks for an inline wikilink at the first natural mention.

## Changed

- **Questions must be self-contained.** The prompt now requires every stem to define its variables and restate the problem setup it reasons about — you should be able to answer from concepts plus the stem, never from remembering the note. Numeric instances must be non-trivial: values a learner can answer from familiarity (4th root of 81) are ruled out unless triviality is the trap.
- State-tracing questions must cross-check their answer against every fact the stem asserts before shipping.

## Fixed

- Bare `==` comparisons outside code (e.g. `arr[low]==arr[mid]==arr[high]`) rendered as yellow highlight in Obsidian (`==text==` is highlight syntax). Prose tokens containing `==` are now backtick-wrapped mechanically, and the prompt warns the model about the collision.
- A time-dependent test fixture (practice-draft expiry) that started failing once its hardcoded date aged past the 7-day draft window.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Fixed

- **Claude output-token truncation, actually fixed.** 0.4.1 chunked large sessions, but the real culprit was elsewhere: newer Claude models (Sonnet 5 and later) run *adaptive thinking by default* when no `thinking` parameter is sent, and thinking tokens count against `max_tokens` — so the model silently spent the output budget thinking and truncated the question JSON, even on small batches. Question generation now explicitly disables thinking (the schema already forces reasoning before each answer via the explanation field, so nothing is lost), with a self-healing retry for models that reject the setting.
- Always-on-thinking models (Claude Fable/Mythos family) can't disable thinking, so they get extra `max_tokens` headroom (16K) instead.
- All Anthropic generation requests now floor `max_tokens` at 8K regardless of batch size — it's a cap, not a spend, so unusually long questions no longer truncate.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
### Large sessions no longer hit output token limits

- A 20-question request could exceed the output ceiling shared by every provider (~8K tokens), truncating mid-JSON — reason-rich options simply don't fit in one response. Sessions above 8 questions now generate in **balanced chunks** (e.g. 7 + 7 + 6) behind the scenes and merge, so no single request can truncate, on any provider. Per-question output budget also increased to match the richer option format.

### Daily practice always blends old and new

- Previously, a review backlog could fill the entire daily topic limit and shut new notes out completely. Daily sessions now **reserve up to two slots for never-practiced notes** whenever they exist — you stay in touch with new material every day, and the displaced reviews remain due and come back tomorrow. The existing cap (at most three new notes once reviews exist) still protects review debt from novelty.
- The **daily topic limit now goes up to 30** (was 12). Note that every selected note's content is sent to the model, so higher limits increase per-session token use.

### Under the hood

- Chunked generation reuses the flow engine's batch machinery, so every chunk still passes the full calibration, difficulty-verification, and repair pipeline, and duplicate stems across chunks are filtered.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
The research-grounding release: adaptive flow sessions, real spaced-repetition scheduling, and question quality that works for any subject — not just the vault it was developed in.

### Flow sessions (new)

- Sessions now start after a small opening batch and generate the rest **in the background while you answer**, adapting to how the session is going. A rolling controller keeps your success rate near the ~80–85% band that learning research says maximizes both retention and engagement — stepping difficulty up after fast, accurate streaks and easing off after misses, never whiplashing.
- Every follow-up batch knows what was already asked, so nothing repeats within a session. If generation fails mid-session, the session simply ends at the questions you have. Turn "Adaptive flow generation" off in settings to generate everything up front.

### Question types

- **Select-all-that-apply questions** (new): 4–5 options with two or more correct answers, graded as an exact set (order never matters), with a "Select all that apply" label, per-option correct/incorrect highlighting, and A–E keyboard toggling.
- Integer and decimal answers continue to grade with sensible tolerance, and the model is now told to prefer them whenever the answer is a number you should compute rather than recognize.

### Scheduling on real memory science

- Review intervals follow an FSRS-style forgetting curve, and every **subtopic now carries its own memory strength** — a note whose weakest concept is fading comes back before the note as a whole is due, with the reason shown.
- Editing a practiced note partially resets its stability (new content = partially invalidated memory) and brings it back soon.
- Sessions interleave questions across notes, at most three never-practiced notes join a daily session once you have review history, and a **"Review intensity"** setting (80–95% target recall) controls how aggressively things come back.
- Your streak now shows in the daily reminder and in a clickable status-bar item (`Practice 🔥 3d · 2 due`).

### Question quality, for any subject

- The generation prompt no longer carries any subject-specific assumptions — depth is derived from what mastery means in *your* note's field (procedural, quantitative, conceptual, or factual material), so history notes get meaningful fact questions while technical notes get construct/predict/debug questions.
- A **"Practice intent"** setting (durable mastery / exam cram / broad review) shapes question style to what you're studying for.
- Deterministic guards reject answer-leaking stems, near-duplicate options, and re-skinned batches that repeat one scenario; distractors must each embody a specific, nameable mistake.

### Consistency and cost

- Strict structured output on every provider that supports it (OpenAI, OpenRouter, Gemini), with automatic graceful fallback when a gateway rejects the schema.
- A format exemplar plus math-brace repair keep LaTeX, code blocks, and options rendering uniformly across models.
- Output tokens now scale with the question count instead of always paying the maximum, and Anthropic requests cache the shared system prompt.

### Fixes and polish

- Keyboard shortcuts only act when the practice pane is active — typing in another note can no longer select answers.
- Vault edits refresh the practice index within about 30 seconds instead of waiting for the half-hour sweep.
- Removed an orphaned legacy quiz UI; new "Export practice dataset" command writes your accumulated learning signals as JSONL.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
### Improved

- Tightened the target difficulty mix so high-skill notes stop receiving easy-heavy daily sessions.
- Added a provider-agnostic generation loop that retries under-challenging or underfilled batches before accepting them.

### Fixed

- Repaired non-JSON fenced provider output before parsing, including language-tag mistakes that previously broke question generation.
- Preserved harder surplus questions during flow balancing so stronger replacements are not discarded too early.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
## Release notes

- Redesigned the full practice session view with the Figma-style question layout, answer cards, result panel, skip/completion dialogs, feedback controls, and bottom action bar.
- Reworked correct, incorrect, skipped, save, stopwatch, and feedback visuals to use Obsidian theme variables for light and dark mode.
- Added animation guards so hover/motion effects respect reduced-motion preferences.
- Polished the unfinished-session resume notice styling.
- Improved question parsing so non-JSON fenced blocks are skipped before parsing generated question payloads.

## Verification

- `npm run check` passed locally.
- GitHub Actions passed for the `main` push and the `0.3.5` release tag.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
### Practice Quality
- Added deterministic MCQ option shuffling after model output, so the model no longer controls whether the correct answer is usually A/B/C/D.
- Preserved terminal choices such as `All of the above` and `None of the above` at the end while still shuffling the substantive options.
- Added a source-aware validation gate for named problem notes, so generated questions must restate the needed input/goal/setup instead of expecting the learner to remember a problem statement from the note title.
- Improved difficulty calibration so direct branch checks, one-step recall, and inflated hard labels are downgraded or rejected before the quiz starts.

### Daily Practice
- Added daily streak credit that only counts meaningful daily practice, not skips or speed-clicked sessions.
- Kept manual and extra practice useful without adding duplicate streak days.
- Mixed due review with a limited number of untouched topics, so large vaults introduce new material bit by bit instead of flooding the session.
- Added dashboard/result details explaining whether daily practice counted and why.

### Flow and Scheduling
- Improved flow navigation so correct easy answers step up to medium, repeated correct medium answers can pull hard questions forward, and misses/skips recover to easier questions.
- Added warm-up/stretch session calibration from note memory, recent fluency, and fragile topics.
- Added bounded question-quality feedback capture (`Too easy`, `Too hard`, `Bad concept`) and used it as future prompt calibration for the same topics.

### Notes, Media, and Providers
- Improved prompt construction from note structure, frontmatter, aliases, headings, hidden concepts, subtopic memory, images, SVGs, remote media, and PDFs.
- Added prompt budget controls for huge notes and large manual selections, prioritizing weak or relevant sections.
- Hardened provider support across Gemini, Anthropic, OpenAI Responses, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Qwen, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
- Moved provider keys to Obsidian secret storage and added migration for legacy saved keys/model defaults.

### UX and Reliability
- Preserved unfinished practice sessions across reloads and added confirmation before discarding generated questions.
- Fixed dashboard/sidebar restore after reload and kept dashboard state stable through workspace layout churn.
- Improved Markdown/code rendering in questions, answers, explanations, and saved practice history.
- Added cancellation and clearer notices while questions are generating.

### Tests
- Expanded adaptive core coverage to 111 tests across parsing, option shuffling, title-context validation, flow behavior, daily streaks, note indexing, provider handling, prompt budgets, media handling, and release metadata.
- Verified `npm run check` passes for the 0.3.0 release metadata.

releases.author: github-actions[bot]
releases.description:
### Daily Practice
- Added the Adaptive Practice dashboard with daily review status, due-note counts, scan state, and a streak display.
- Added daily practice reminders with retry behavior, reminder suppression for the day, and extra practice after the daily session.
- Added skill/spaced-review scheduling based on note memory, due dates, recent changes, fluency, skipped answers, and low-skill topics.

### Practice Experience
- Added generated practice sessions with modal and full-tab views, progress navigation, answer review, and skill-change summaries.
- Added unfinished-session drafts so generated questions and answers can survive reloads until the session is completed or discarded.
- Added flow-aware question ordering and challenge top-ups so sessions ramp from approachable questions toward transfer.
- Added confirmation before replacing or discarding unfinished generated sessions.

### Notes, Media, and Providers
- Added structured note reading from frontmatter, titles, aliases, headings, sections, tags, links, and cleaned note text.
- Added prompt support for local images, SVG text, remote images, PDFs, media captions, and attachment budgets.
- Added BYOK model providers for Gemini, Anthropic, OpenAI Responses, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Qwen, and OpenAI-compatible endpoints.
- Added Obsidian secret storage support with migration away from legacy saved API keys.

### Quality and Reliability
- Added question parsing/validation for JSON responses, MCQ options, numeric answers, Markdown/code rendering, and provider error guidance.
- Added source-topic reconciliation so generated questions map back to selected notes even when titles, aliases, or paths differ.
- Added dashboard/sidebar reload restoration and practice-view lifecycle controls.
- Added the release workflow that builds plugin artifacts and creates draft GitHub releases from tags.

### Tests
- Added adaptive core coverage for provider handling, note cleaning, media parsing, prompt budgets, scheduling, flow ordering, drafts, reminders, grading, and release metadata.

releases.author: anupchavan
releases.description: Alpha Release
